construct_description: The ability to plan, execute, monitor, and close projects effectively — including scope definition, scheduling, risk management, stakeholder communication, and performance control.

dimensions.name: Planning
dimensions.description: Scope definition, WBS, scheduling, milestones, and resource planning

dimensions.name: Risk Management
dimensions.description: Risk identification, assessment, mitigation strategies, and contingency planning

dimensions.name: Stakeholder Communication
dimensions.description: Communication plans, stakeholder mapping, and expectation management

dimensions.name: Execution & Control
dimensions.description: Change management, issue tracking, performance measurement, and earned value

persona.name: Morgan
persona.tone: Direct, organized, practical — like a seasoned PM who's seen everything

intro_messages: Hey! I've been wanting to pick your brain about project management — mind if I throw a few scenarios at you? Just respond how you'd actually handle things, no textbook answers needed.
Alright, I want to chat PM with you for a bit. Some of these will be real-world scenarios, some concept checks. Just respond like you would in an actual work conversation.
Quick check-in on your PM instincts — I'll throw some situations at you and want to hear how you'd think through them. No pressure, just a conversation.

messages: You're handed a project with a vague ask: 'build us a new reporting dashboard.' What do you do first before you write a single line of a project plan?
New project, week one — the sponsor says 'we need a customer portal by Q3.' Walk me through your first moves. What are you figuring out before anything else?
A project just landed on your plate and the brief is pretty thin. What does your first week look like in terms of getting organized? What questions are you asking?
acknowledgments: Good instincts — the early scoping work really does set the tone for everything that follows.
Exactly right — a lot of projects get into trouble because people skip this phase and dive straight into execution.
That's the right thinking. Getting clarity upfront is worth way more than moving fast on the wrong thing.

messages: Do you know what a Work Breakdown Structure is and why it matters? Like could you explain it to a new project coordinator?
WBS — can you tell me what that is and when you'd actually use one on a real project?
Scope creep is the silent project killer. How do you prevent it during the planning phase? What tools or practices help?
acknowledgments: Yep — the WBS is one of those foundational tools that looks simple but does a lot of heavy lifting.
Right — breaking work down into manageable pieces is where a lot of project clarity actually comes from.
Exactly. Scope creep prevention really starts in planning, not execution.

messages: You've got 6 tasks that all need to happen but some depend on others. How do you figure out the project schedule and spot the critical path?
Walk me through how you'd build a realistic project timeline. What factors are you considering beyond just task durations?
Your project has a hard deadline that feels tight. How do you approach scheduling to figure out if it's actually achievable?
acknowledgments: Good — scheduling is one part math and one part negotiation, and knowing the critical path is essential.
Exactly right — a timeline that doesn't account for dependencies and resource constraints isn't really a plan.
That's the right way to approach it. Working backward from a deadline and identifying the critical path is key.

low: figure it out as you go

messages: How do you approach risk on a project? Walk me through how you'd identify and document risks at the start of a new project.
Your project has a key vendor who's only 70% reliable. How do you factor that into your risk planning?
What does a solid risk management process actually look like in practice? Not the theory — what do you actually do?
acknowledgments: Solid approach — proactive risk identification is what separates projects that survive surprises from ones that get derailed by them.
Right — that kind of dependency risk needs to be on the register with a real mitigation plan, not just acknowledged.
Good — risk management that actually works is usually pretty systematic and happens early and often.

mid: think about what could go wrong
low: deal with it when it happens

messages: Two weeks before launch, a key team member quits. This was identified as a risk. Walk me through how you'd handle it.
Your project is on track until a critical third-party API changes with no warning and breaks your integration. What's your response?
An issue just became a full-blown crisis on your project — something you sort of saw coming. How do you respond and what do you do differently going forward?
acknowledgments: Good — responding to a materialized risk is different from managing a new issue, and having the contingency plan ready matters.
Exactly — this is where risk planning pays off. Having a documented response plan versus scrambling is night and day.
Right. The 'we saw it coming' moment is painful but also the best learning opportunity for your risk process.

messages: What's the difference between a risk and an issue in PM terms? And how do you track each of them?
Someone on your team says 'I don't want to document risks — it makes the project look bad to stakeholders.' How do you respond?
Do you use a risk matrix? Walk me through how you'd score and prioritize risks on a project.
acknowledgments: Exactly — that distinction matters more than people think, especially for how you respond and communicate.
Right answer — transparent risk management builds more trust with stakeholders than pretending problems don't exist.
Good — the probability x impact scoring approach is the industry standard for a reason.

mid: risk is something that might happen
issue is something that already happened

messages: You've got a project with 15+ stakeholders — some technical, some exec, some end users. How do you manage communications so everyone gets what they need?
A senior stakeholder is getting too involved in day-to-day decisions and it's slowing the team down. How do you handle that?
Walk me through how you'd build a communication plan for a medium-complexity project. What goes in it?
acknowledgments: Good — a tailored communication approach by audience is one of those PM basics that makes a huge practical difference.
Exactly right — managing stakeholder involvement levels is a real PM skill, and having that conversation diplomatically is key.
Solid — a communication plan that actually gets used is specific enough to tell people when, what, and how they'll be informed.

messages: Your project is two weeks behind schedule and you need to tell the sponsor. How do you approach that conversation?
A stakeholder is convinced the project should do something that's out of scope. They're senior. How do you handle it?
You're about to give a project status update to the executive team and things aren't going great. How do you prepare and what do you say?
acknowledgments: Right — delivering bad news proactively and with a plan is much better than waiting or softening the message too much.
Exactly — scope protection with senior stakeholders is tricky, but the change control process is your friend here.
Good approach — execs generally want the summary, the impact, and what you're doing about it. They don't need the detail.

messages: Two key stakeholders have conflicting priorities for your project — the CMO wants feature A first, the CTO wants feature B. You can't do both. What do you do?
A stakeholder tells you they hate the direction of the project at the 60% mark. How do you manage that situation?
What does good stakeholder expectation management look like throughout a project — not just at kickoff?
acknowledgments: Good — stakeholder conflicts don't resolve themselves and the PM is usually the one who has to facilitate the alignment.
Exactly — a late stakeholder concern is way better than finding out post-launch. The key is validating the concern and figuring out the real ask.
Right — expectation management is ongoing, not a one-time conversation. Regular alignment checkpoints are the real answer.

messages: Mid-project, someone asks to add a significant new feature that wasn't in the original scope. How do you handle that request?
Scope creep is happening on your project — small additions keep getting approved informally. How do you get it back under control?
What does a solid change management process look like on a real project? Walk me through the steps.
acknowledgments: Exactly — even reasonable requests need to go through change control. Ad-hoc scope additions are how timelines and budgets blow up.
Right — informal approvals are where scope creep lives. Formalizing the process is the only way to stop it.
Good — change management isn't about saying no, it's about making the cost and impact visible before the decision is made.

messages: How do you track whether a project is actually on track? What metrics or methods do you use to measure performance?
Have you ever heard of Earned Value Management? What is it and why would you use it?
Your project is burning budget faster than planned but deliverables are on schedule. Is that a problem? How do you analyze it?
acknowledgments: Good — tracking both schedule and cost performance together is much more powerful than looking at either in isolation.
Exactly — EVM gives you a much clearer picture than 'budget spent vs planned' because it factors in what you actually accomplished.
Right — that's a real red flag. Ahead on deliverables but over on budget means you may have front-loaded the work or have a cost variance that will compound.

messages: Last one, and be honest — where would you rate your PM skills right now? 1 (just heard of Gantt charts) to 10 (PMP-certified and proud). Where do you actually land?
Final question: if you had to self-assess your project management knowledge honestly, what would you say? What do you feel solid on and where are the gaps?
Alright, wrap-up: how would you grade yourself on PM fundamentals right now? Be specific if you can — what's your strongest area and where do you know you need work?
acknowledgments: Got it — let me run through what we covered and see what the picture looks like...
Noted. Okay, give me a moment to pull this together...
Good self-awareness. Let me see how that lines up with what we discussed...

overall_labels.text: Seriously strong PM fundamentals — you think like a seasoned project manager 🏆
Solid across the board — you've got the core frameworks and know how to apply them 📋
Good foundation — strong instincts with a few areas worth sharpening 💪
Decent grasp of the basics, some key gaps — targeted practice would pay off here 📈
You're familiar with project work but the formal PM framework is still developing — solid starting point 🔧
Early days on PM knowledge — good news is these fundamentals are very learnable with the right resources 📚

feedback_templates.high: Strong command here — you clearly understand both the theory and how it applies in real project situations.
feedback_templates.mid: Good instincts. Tightening up the formal frameworks in this area would make your approach even more consistent.
feedback_templates.low: This is a growth area — consider digging into PMBOK basics or a Project+ prep resource to fill in the foundation.
